text: Exercise-induced dehydration up to 4% body weight loss does not significantly alter cycling time-trial performance during outdoor exercise conditions compared with maintaining euhydration.
Drinking according to thirst sensation during exercise maximises cycling time-trial performance compared to drinking below or above thirst levels.
Drinking to thirst produces a 5.2% improvement in time-trial performance compared to drinking below thirst levels.
Drinking to thirst produces a 2.4% improvement in time-trial performance compared to drinking above thirst levels (overdrinking), though this difference was not statistically significant.
Exercise intensity and duration have a much greater impact on cycling time-trial performance than exercise-induced dehydration.
The probability that drinking to thirst confers a real and meaningful advantage over under-drinking during field-condition time trials is approximately 98%.
